title: Dowï؟½s Flat
content:
آ آ آ آ Dowï؟½s flat. Thatï؟½s its name;
آ آ آ آ And I reckon that you
آ آ آ آ Are a stranger? The same?
آ آ آ آ Well, I thought it was true,
آ آ آ آ For thar isnï؟½t a man on the river as canï؟½t spot the place at first view.

آ آ آ آ It was called after Dow,
آ آ آ آ Which the same was an ass,
آ آ آ آ And as to the how
آ آ آ آ Thet the thing kem to pass,
آ آ آ آ Jest tie up your hoss to that buckeye, and sit ye down here in the grass.

آ آ آ آ You see this ï؟½yer Dow
آ آ آ آ Hed the worst kind of luck;
آ آ آ آ He slipped up somehow
آ آ آ آ On each thing thet he struck.
آ آ آ آ Why, ef heï؟½d a straddled thet fence-rail, the derned thingï؟½d get up and buck.

آ آ آ آ He mined on the bar
آ آ آ آ Till he couldnï؟½t pay rates;
آ آ آ آ He was smashed by a car
آ آ آ آ When he tunneled with Bates;
آ آ آ آ And right on the top of his trouble kem his wife and five kids from the States.

آ آ آ آ It was rough, mighty rough;
آ آ آ آ But the boys they stood by,
آ آ آ آ And they brought him the stuff
آ آ آ آ For a house, on the sly;
آ آ آ آ And the old woman, well, she did washing, and took on when no one was nigh.

آ آ آ آ But this ï؟½yer luck of Dowï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Was so powerful mean
آ آ آ آ That the spring near his house
آ آ آ آ Dried right up on the green;
آ آ آ آ And he sunk forty feet down for water, but nary a drop to be seen.

آ آ آ آ Then the bar petered out,
آ آ آ آ And the boys wouldnï؟½t stay;
آ آ آ آ And the chills got about,
آ آ آ آ And his wife fell away;
آ آ آ آ But Dow in his well kept a pegginï؟½ in his usual ridikilous way.

آ آ آ آ One day, it was June,
آ آ آ آ And a year ago, jest
آ آ آ آ This Dow kem at noon
آ آ آ آ To his work like the rest,
آ آ آ آ With a shovel and pick on his shoulder, and derringer hid in his breast.

آ آ آ آ He goes to the well,
آ آ آ آ And he stands on the brink,
آ آ آ آ And stops for a spell
آ آ آ آ Jest to listen and think:
آ آ آ آ For the sun in his eyes (jest like this, sir!), you see, kinder made the cuss blink.

آ آ آ آ His two ragged gals
آ آ آ آ In the gulch were at play,
آ آ آ آ And a gownd that was Salï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Kinder flapped on a bay:
آ آ آ آ Not much for a man to be leavinï؟½, but his all, as Iï؟½ve heerï؟½d the folks say.

آ آ آ آ And Thatï؟½s a peart hoss
آ آ آ آ Thet youï؟½ve got, ainï؟½t it now?
آ آ آ آ What might be her cost?
آ آ آ آ Eh? Oh! Well, then, Dow
آ آ آ آ Letï؟½s see, well, that forty-foot grave wasnï؟½t his, sir, that day, anyhow.

آ آ آ آ For a blow of his pick
آ آ آ آ Sorter caved in the side,
آ آ آ آ And he looked and turned sick,
آ آ آ آ Then he trembled and cried.
آ آ آ آ For you see the dern cuss had struck ï؟½Water?ï؟½ Beg your parding, young man, there you lied!

آ آ آ آ It was gold, in the quartz,
آ آ آ آ And it ran all alike;
آ آ آ آ And I reckon five oughts
آ آ آ آ Was the worth of that strike;
آ آ آ آ And that house with the coopilowï؟½s hisï؟½n, which the same isnï؟½t bad for a Pike.

آ آ آ آ Thetï؟½s why itï؟½s Dowï؟½s Flat;
آ آ آ آ And the thing of it is
آ آ آ آ That he kinder got that
آ آ آ آ Through sheer contrairiness:
آ آ آ آ For ï؟½twas water the derned cuss was seekinï؟½, and his luck made him certain to miss.

آ آ آ آ Thetï؟½s so! Tharï؟½s your way,
آ آ آ آ To the left of yon tree;
آ آ آ آ But a look hï؟½yur, say?
آ آ آ آ Wonï؟½t you come up to tea?
آ آ آ آ No? Well, then the next time youï؟½re passinï؟½; and ask after Dow,آ آ آ آ and thetï؟½s me.
meaning:
Bret Harte (Francis)'s contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Dowï؟½s Flat"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
